rationale: The citing paper uses He, Inman, and Mittal (2008) in core hypothesis development, explicitly drawing a parallel asymmetric pattern (effect of self-efficacy for one identity group but not the other) and linking it to upside focus in financial decisions. It also relies on that paper for operational choices, including the investment-task design and self-efficacy measurement logic based on prior performance. Because the cited study informs both conceptual framing and multiple study methods, this is more than a passing background mention.
relation_description: The citing paper extends He et al. (2008) from a gender-based framework to a political-identity/SDO framework in financial risk taking, preserving the core moderated pattern around self-efficacy and upside focus. It also adapts He et al.'s task and measurement approaches in several studies.
